skill.name: newsletter-curation
skill.description: Newsletter curation with content sourcing, editorial structure, and subscriber growth strategies. Covers issue formatting, link roundups, commentary style, and sending cadence. Use for: email newsletters, link roundups, weekly digests, curated content, creator newsletters. Triggers: newsletter, email newsletter, newsletter curation, weekly digest, link roundup, curated newsletter, newsletter writing, newsletter format, subscriber growth, newsletter strategy, content curation, newsletter template
skill.summary: Newsletter curation with content sourcing, editorial structure, and subscriber growth strategies. Co...
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: All 31 static findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The skill is a documentation guide for newsletter curation using the inference.sh CLI. External command patterns are markdown code examples showing CLI usage, not executable code. Network patterns are legitimate service URLs. No cryptographic code or malicious patterns exist.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Examples Misidentified as Shell Commands
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at 21 locations. These are markdown code blocks showing CLI command examples (infsh app run, curl installation, npx skills add). This is documentation, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: These are markdown fenced code blocks (```bash ... ```) showing CLI command examples for users. The static scanner incorrectly interpreted documentation examples as actual code execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Legitimate Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: Static scanner detected 'Hardcoded URL' at 4 locations. These URLs point to inference.sh service endpoints required for the CLI tool to function (installation script, download locations).
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URLs like https://inference.sh and https://cli.inference.sh are legitimate service endpoints for the inference.sh CLI tool. These are required for tool installation and usage, not sensitive data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Standard CLI Installation Pattern
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 'Pipe to shell pattern' at line 14. The command 'curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh' is a standard installation pattern used by many legitimate CLI tools (like Docker, Node.js, etc.).
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is a standard, widely-used installation method for CLI tools. The skill includes a note explaining the install script only detects OS/architecture and verifies SHA-256 checksum.

content.value_statement: Creating consistent, high-quality newsletters takes significant time and effort. This skill provides curated content sourcing strategies, proven newsletter formats, and growth tactics to help you build an engaged subscriber base.
actual_capabilities: Guide users through newsletter creation using inference.sh CLI tools
Provide proven newsletter formats (link roundup, deep dive, essay, Q&A, data trends)
Offer content sourcing strategies from news, research, blogs, and social sources
Give editorial structure templates for consistent, professional issues
Share subscriber growth strategies and metrics that matter
Help craft compelling subject lines under 50 characters
limitations: Does not actually send newsletters - provides guidance only
Requires inference.sh CLI tool for some content generation features
Does not provide email sending infrastructure or analytics
Does not create actual images - provides HTML templates for image generation

use_cases.title: Tech Newsletter Creator
use_cases.description: A developer or tech enthusiast wants to start a weekly tech newsletter. The skill guides them through content sourcing, format selection, and growth strategies.

use_cases.title: Content Marketer
use_cases.description: A content marketer needs to create a company newsletter. The skill provides professional templates and editorial structures for consistent brand messaging.

use_cases.title: Newsletter Consultant
use_cases.description: A consultant helps clients improve their existing newsletters. The skill offers frameworks for analyzing and optimizing newsletter performance.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Newsletter Format
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a weekly newsletter about [topic]. Show me the standard structure and format I should use.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner looking for basic newsletter structure

prompt_templates.title: Content Sourcing Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the best sources for finding high-quality content to include in my [niche] newsletter? Give me specific platforms and search strategies.

prompt_templates.title: Subject Line Optimization
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate 10 subject line options for my newsletter issue about [topic]. Keep them under 50 characters and make them compelling.

prompt_templates.title: Growth Strategy Development
prompt_templates.prompt: My newsletter has [number] subscribers but growth has stalled. Analyze my current approach and suggest specific tactics to grow my audience.

output_examples.input: Help me create a weekly tech newsletter
output_examples.output:
Here is a proven newsletter structure:

## Issue #1: [Topic] — [Date]

### Hello
2-3 sentences about what's on your mind and what this issue covers.

### The Big Story
Your deepest analysis or most important piece with 2-3 sentence commentary.

### Worth Reading
- **[Title](url)** — Your take on why this matters
- **[Title](url)** — Brief commentary

### Quick Hits
- One-liner + link
- One-liner + link

---
That's it. Forward to a colleague who would enjoy this.

[Your name]

output_examples.input: Give me subject lines for a Python newsletter
output_examples.output:
Here are 10 subject lines under 50 characters:

1. #47: The Python trick nobody knows
2. 5 Python mistakes I made this month
3. Is Python still the best choice?
4. This week in Python: v4.0 rumors
5. The Python library that changed everything
6. Python vs Go: The final verdict
7. 3 Python tips I wish I knew earlier
8. Why I switched back to Python
9. Python announces major changes
10. The Python tutorial you needed

best_practices: Maintain consistent schedule - same day, same time every week
Always add your perspective to curated links - just linking is not enough
Keep subject lines under 50 characters for mobile readability
anti_patterns: Sending without a consistent schedule - readers will forget about you
Including links without personal commentary - you become just a bookmark service
Using generic subject lines like 'Weekly Newsletter' or 'This Week's Updates'

faq.question: How often should I send my newsletter?
faq.answer: Weekly is the sweet spot for most newsletters. It builds a predictable habit for readers. Send on Tuesday or Thursday for highest open rates.

faq.question: How many links should I include per issue?
faq.answer: Aim for 5-10 curated picks maximum. Too many links become overwhelming and nothing stands out. Quality over quantity.

faq.question: What is the best day to send a newsletter?
faq.answer: Tuesday has the highest open rates, followed by Thursday. Avoid Monday (inbox overload) and Friday (weekend mode).

faq.question: How long should my newsletter be?
faq.answer: It depends on the format. Link roundups can be shorter. Deep dives and essays can be 500-1000 words. Focus on quality over length.

faq.question: How do I grow my subscriber base?
faq.answer: The best strategy is consistent quality - be worth reading. Cross-promote with complementary newsletters, post on social media, and add a clear subscribe CTA.

faq.question: What metrics should I track?
faq.answer: Focus on open rate (30-40% is good, 40%+ is great) and click rate (3-5% is good). Reply rate and forward rate indicate engagement.
